The physics behind MRI are just wild. We aren't using radiation at all, right?

Exactly. The human body is mostly water, meaning it is packed with hydrogen atoms.
When you slide into the tube of an MRI scanner, you are entering a magnetic field

That is equal parts terrifying and incredible. And that massive magnetic field forces all those

like millions of compass needles, all suddenly pointing true north.
Right. And once they are aligned, the MRI machine fires a specific pulse of

But the radio pulse is brief. When it turns off, the protons snap back into alignment with
the main magnetic field. And as they return to their baseline, they emit a faint radio signal
of their own. And the scanner is essentially a giant radio antenna listening for that echo.
Exactly.

Because of the path of least resistance. Water diffuses randomly in an open space.
